abstract: We report the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions, such as BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻, into the redox-active metal-organic framework Fe₂(dobpdc) (dobpdc⁴⁻ = 4,4′-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3′-dicarboxylate). This reaction causes only a slight contraction of the host lattice and triggers the appearance of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band, signifying enhanced charge mobility within the framework. Our investigation reveals that while both the metal centers and organic ligands can be oxidized, only the metal-based oxidation is fully reversible, proceeding stoichiometrically under both chemical and electrochemical conditions. Electrochemical studies demonstrate that nanoconfinement effects impose strong dependencies on anion size and solvent choice during the insertion process. Notably, the reversible anion insertion capability of Fe₂(dobpdc) allowed us to develop a prototype dual-ion battery using a sodium anode. As a cathode, the material exhibits an exceptionally high initial reduction potential and maintains stability over at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, achieving a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
